html:
XI

The Fat Man

The telephone bell was ringing when Spade returned to his office after sending Brigid O‚ÄôShaughnessy off to Effie Perine‚Äôs house. He went to the telephone.

‚ÄúHello.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Yes, this is Spade.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Yes, I got it. I‚Äôve been waiting to hear from you.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Who?‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Mr.¬ÝGutman? Oh, yes, sure!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Now‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe sooner the better.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ 12-C.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Right. Say fifteen minutes.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Right.‚Äù

Spade sat on the corner of his desk beside the telephone and rolled a cigarette. His mouth was a hard complacent v. His eyes, watching his fingers make the cigarette, smoldered over lower lids drawn up straight.

The door opened and Iva Archer came in.

Spade said, ‚ÄúHello, honey,‚Äù in a voice as lightly amiable as his face had suddenly become.

‚ÄúOh, Sam, forgive me! forgive me!‚Äù she cried in a choked voice. She stood just inside the door, wadding a black-bordered handkerchief in her small gloved hands, peering into his face with frightened red and swollen eyes.

He did not get up from his seat on the desk-corner. He said: ‚ÄúSure. That‚Äôs all right. Forget it.‚Äù

‚ÄúBut, Sam,‚Äù she wailed, ‚ÄúI sent those policemen there. I was mad, crazy with jealousy, and I phoned them that if they‚Äôd go there they‚Äôd learn something about Miles‚Äôs murder.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat made you think that?‚Äù

‚ÄúOh, I didn‚Äôt! But I was mad, Sam, and I wanted to hurt you.‚Äù

‚ÄúIt made things damned awkward.‚Äù He put his arm around her and drew her nearer. ‚ÄúBut it‚Äôs all right now, only don‚Äôt get any more crazy notions like that.‚Äù

‚ÄúI won‚Äôt,‚Äù she promised, ‚Äúever. But you weren‚Äôt nice to me last night. You were cold and distant and wanted to get rid of me, when I had come down there and waited so long to warn you, and you‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúWarn me about what?‚Äù

‚ÄúAbout Phil. He‚Äôs found out about‚ÅÝ‚Äîabout you being in love with me, and Miles had told him about my wanting a divorce, though of course he never knew what for, and now Phil thinks we‚ÅÝ‚Äîyou killed his brother because he wouldn‚Äôt give me the divorce so we could get married. He told me he believed that, and yesterday he went and told the police.‚Äù

‚ÄúThat‚Äôs nice,‚Äù Spade said softly. ‚ÄúAnd you came to warn me, and because I was busy you got up on your ear and helped this damned Phil Archer stir things up.‚Äù

‚ÄúI‚Äôm sorry,‚Äù she whimpered, ‚ÄúI know you won‚Äôt forgive me. I‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI‚Äôm sorry, sorry, sorry.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou ought to be,‚Äù he agreed, ‚Äúon your own account as well as mine. Has Dundy been to see you since Phil did his talking? Or anybody from the bureau?‚Äù

‚ÄúNo.‚Äù Alarm opened her eyes and mouth.

‚ÄúThey will,‚Äù he said, ‚Äúand it‚Äôd be just as well to not let them find you here. Did you tell them who you were when you phoned?‚Äù

‚ÄúOh, no! I simply told them that if they‚Äôd go to your apartment right away they‚Äôd learn something about the murder and hung up.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhere‚Äôd you phone from?‚Äù

‚ÄúThe drugstore up above your place. Oh, Sam, dearest, I‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù

He patted her shoulder and said pleasantly: ‚ÄúIt was a dumb trick, all right, but it‚Äôs done now. You‚Äôd better run along home and think up things to tell the police. You‚Äôll be hearing from them. Maybe it‚Äôd be best to say ‚Äòno‚Äô right across the board.‚Äù He frowned at something distant. ‚ÄúOr maybe you‚Äôd better see Sid Wise first.‚Äù He removed his arm from around her, took a card out of his pocket, scribbled three lines on its back, and gave it to her. ‚ÄúYou can tell Sid everything.‚Äù He frowned. ‚ÄúOr almost everything. Where were you the night Miles was shot?‚Äù

‚ÄúHome,‚Äù she replied without hesitating.

He shook his head, grinning at her.

‚ÄúI was,‚Äù she insisted.

‚ÄúNo,‚Äù he said, ‚Äúbut if that‚Äôs your story it‚Äôs all right with me. Go see Sid. It‚Äôs up on the next corner, the pinkish building, room eight-twenty-seven.‚Äù

Her blue eyes tried to probe his yellow-grey ones. ‚ÄúWhat makes you think I wasn‚Äôt home?‚Äù she asked slowly.

‚ÄúNothing except that I know you weren‚Äôt.‚Äù

‚ÄúBut I was, I was.‚Äù Her lips twisted and anger darkened her eyes. ‚ÄúEffie Perine told you that,‚Äù she said indignantly. ‚ÄúI saw her looking at my clothes and snooping around. You know she doesn‚Äôt like me, Sam. Why do you believe things she tells you when you know she‚Äôd do anything to make trouble for me?‚Äù

‚ÄúJesus, you women,‚Äù Spade said mildly. He looked at the watch on his wrist. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôll have to trot along, precious. I‚Äôm late for an appointment now. You do what you want, but if I were you I‚Äôd tell Sid the truth or nothing. I mean leave out the parts you don‚Äôt want to tell him, but don‚Äôt make up anything to take its place.‚Äù

‚ÄúI‚Äôm not lying to you, Sam,‚Äù she protested.

‚ÄúLike hell you‚Äôre not,‚Äù he said and stood up.

She strained on tiptoe to hold her face nearer his. ‚ÄúYou don‚Äôt believe me?‚Äù she whispered.

‚ÄúI don‚Äôt believe you.‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd you won‚Äôt forgive me for‚ÅÝ‚Äîfor what I did?‚Äù

‚ÄúSure I do.‚Äù He bent his head and kissed her mouth. ‚ÄúThat‚Äôs all right. Now run along.‚Äù

She put her arms around him. ‚ÄúWon‚Äôt you go with me to see Mr.¬ÝWise?‚Äù

‚ÄúI can‚Äôt, and I‚Äôd only be in the way.‚Äù He patted her arms, took them from around his body, and kissed her left wrist between glove and sleeve. He put his hands on her shoulders, turned her to face the door, and released her with a little push. ‚ÄúBeat it,‚Äù he ordered.

The mahogany door of suite 12-C at the Alexandria Hotel was opened by the boy Spade had talked to in the Belvedere lobby. Spade said, ‚ÄúHello,‚Äù good-naturedly. The boy did not say anything. He stood aside holding the door open.

Spade went in. A fat man came to meet him.

The fat man was flabbily fat with bulbous pink cheeks and lips and chins and neck, with a great soft egg of a belly that was all his torso, and pendant cones for arms and legs. As he advanced to meet Spade all his bulbs rose and shook and fell separately with each step, in the manner of clustered soap-bubbles not yet released from the pipe through which they had been blown. His eyes, made small by fat puffs around them, were dark and sleek. Dark ringlets thinly covered his broad scalp. He wore a black cutaway coat, black vest, black satin Ascot tie holding a pinkish pearl, striped grey worsted trousers, and patent-leather shoes.

His voice was a throaty purr. ‚ÄúAh, Mr.¬ÝSpade,‚Äù he said with enthusiasm and held out a hand like a fat pink star.

Spade took the hand and smiled and said: ‚ÄúHow do you do, Mr.¬ÝGutman?‚Äù

Holding Spade‚Äôs hand, the fat man turned beside him, put his other hand to Spade‚Äôs elbow, and guided him across a green rug to a green plush chair beside a table that held a siphon, some glasses, and a bottle of Johnnie Walker whiskey on a tray, a box of cigars‚ÅÝ‚ÄîCoronas del Ritz‚ÅÝ‚Äîtwo newspapers, and a small and plain yellow soapstone box.

Spade sat in the green chair. The fat man began to fill two glasses from bottle and siphon. The boy had disappeared. Doors set in three of the room‚Äôs walls were shut. The fourth wall, behind Spade, was pierced by two windows looking out over Geary Street.

‚ÄúWe begin well, sir,‚Äù the fat man purred, turning with a proffered glass in his hand. ‚ÄúI distrust a man that says when. If he‚Äôs got to be careful not to drink too much it‚Äôs because he‚Äôs not to be trusted when he does.‚Äù

Spade took the glass and, smiling, made the beginning of a bow over it.

The fat man raised his glass and held it against a window‚Äôs light. He nodded approvingly at the bubbles running up in it. He said: ‚ÄúWell, sir, here‚Äôs to plain speaking and clear understanding.‚Äù

They drank and lowered their glasses.

The fat man looked shrewdly at Spade and asked: ‚ÄúYou‚Äôre a closemouthed man?‚Äù

Spade shook his head. ‚ÄúI like to talk.‚Äù

‚ÄúBetter and better!‚Äù the fat man exclaimed. ‚ÄúI distrust a closemouthed man. He generally picks the wrong time to talk and says the wrong things. Talking‚Äôs something you can‚Äôt do judiciously unless you keep in practice.‚Äù He beamed over his glass. ‚ÄúWe‚Äôll get along, sir, that we will.‚Äù He set his glass on the table and held the box of Coronas del Ritz out to Spade. ‚ÄúA cigar, sir.‚Äù

Spade took a cigar, trimmed the end of it, and lighted it. Meanwhile the fat man pulled another green plush chair around to face Spade‚Äôs within convenient distance and placed a smoking-stand within reach of both chairs. Then he took his glass from the table, took a cigar from the box, and lowered himself into his chair. His bulbs stopped jouncing and settled into flabby rest. He sighed comfortably and said: ‚ÄúNow, sir, we‚Äôll talk if you like. And I‚Äôll tell you right out that I‚Äôm a man who likes talking to a man that likes to talk.‚Äù

‚ÄúSwell. Will we talk about the black bird?‚Äù

The fat man laughed and his bulbs rode up and down on his laughter. ‚ÄúWill we?‚Äù he asked and, ‚ÄúWe will,‚Äù he replied. His pink face was shiny with delight. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôre the man for me, sir, a man cut along my own lines. No beating about the bush, but right to the point. ‚ÄòWill we talk about the black bird?‚Äô We will. I like that, sir. I like that way of doing business. Let us talk about the black bird by all means, but first, sir, answer me a question, please, though maybe it‚Äôs an unnecessary one, so we‚Äôll understand each other from the beginning. You‚Äôre here as Miss O‚ÄôShaughnessy‚Äôs representative?‚Äù

Spade blew smoke above the fat man‚Äôs head in a long slanting plume. He frowned thoughtfully at the ash-tipped end of his cigar. He replied deliberately: ‚ÄúI can‚Äôt say yes or no. There‚Äôs nothing certain about it either way, yet.‚Äù He looked up at the fat man and stopped frowning. ‚ÄúIt depends.‚Äù

‚ÄúIt depends on‚ÅÝ‚Äî?‚Äù

Spade shook his head. ‚ÄúIf I knew what it depends on I could say yes or no.‚Äù

The fat man took a mouthful from his glass, swallowed it, and suggested: ‚ÄúMaybe it depends on Joel Cairo?‚Äù

Spade‚Äôs prompt ‚ÄúMaybe‚Äù was noncommittal. He drank.

The fat man leaned forward until his belly stopped him. His smile was ingratiating and so was his purring voice. ‚ÄúYou could say, then, that the question is which one of them you‚Äôll represent?‚Äù

‚ÄúYou could put it that way.‚Äù

‚ÄúIt will be one or the other?‚Äù

‚ÄúI didn‚Äôt say that.‚Äù

The fat man‚Äôs eyes glistened. His voice sank to a throaty whisper asking: ‚ÄúWho else is there?‚Äù

Spade pointed his cigar at his own chest. ‚ÄúThere‚Äôs me,‚Äù he said.

The fat man sank back in his chair and let his body go flaccid. He blew his breath out in a long contented gust. ‚ÄúThat‚Äôs wonderful, sir,‚Äù he purred. ‚ÄúThat‚Äôs wonderful. I do like a man that tells you right out he‚Äôs looking out for himself. Don‚Äôt we all? I don‚Äôt trust a man that says he‚Äôs not. And the man that‚Äôs telling the truth when he says he‚Äôs not I distrust most of all, because he‚Äôs an ass and an ass that‚Äôs going contrary to the laws of nature.‚Äù

Spade exhaled smoke. His face was politely attentive. He said: ‚ÄúUh-huh. Now let‚Äôs talk about the black bird.‚Äù

The fat man smiled benevolently. ‚ÄúLet‚Äôs,‚Äù he said. He squinted so that fat puffs crowding together left nothing of his eyes but a dark gleam visible. ‚ÄúMr.¬ÝSpade, have you any conception of how much money can be made out of that black bird?‚Äù

‚ÄúNo.‚Äù

The fat man leaned forward again and put a bloated pink hand on the arm of Spade‚Äôs chair. ‚ÄúWell, sir, if I told you‚ÅÝ‚Äîby Gad, if I told you half!‚ÅÝ‚Äîyou‚Äôd call me a liar.‚Äù

Spade smiled. ‚ÄúNo,‚Äù he said, ‚Äúnot even if I thought it. But if you won‚Äôt take the risk just tell me what it is and I‚Äôll figure out the profits.‚Äù

The fat man laughed. ‚ÄúYou couldn‚Äôt do it, sir. Nobody could do it that hadn‚Äôt had a world of experience with things of that sort, and‚Äù‚ÅÝ‚Äîhe paused impressively‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äúthere aren‚Äôt any other things of that sort.‚Äù His bulbs jostled one another as he laughed again. He stopped laughing, abruptly. His fleshy lips hung open as laughter had left them. He stared at Spade with an intentness that suggested myopia. He asked: ‚ÄúYou mean you don‚Äôt know what it is?‚Äù Amazement took the throatiness out of his voice.

Spade made a careless gesture with his cigar. ‚ÄúOh, hell,‚Äù he said lightly, ‚ÄúI know what it‚Äôs supposed to look like. I know the value in life you people put on it. I don‚Äôt know what it is.‚Äù

‚ÄúShe didn‚Äôt tell you?‚Äù

‚ÄúMiss O‚ÄôShaughnessy?‚Äù

‚ÄúYes. A lovely girl, sir.‚Äù

‚ÄúUh-huh. No.‚Äù

The fat man‚Äôs eyes were dark gleams in ambush behind pink puffs of flesh. He said indistinctly, ‚ÄúShe must know,‚Äù and then, ‚ÄúAnd Cairo didn‚Äôt either?‚Äù

‚ÄúCairo is cagey. He‚Äôs willing to buy it, but he won‚Äôt risk telling me anything I don‚Äôt know already.‚Äù

The fat man moistened his lips with his tongue. ‚ÄúHow much is he willing to buy it for?‚Äù he asked.

‚ÄúTen thousand dollars.‚Äù

The fat man laughed scornfully. ‚ÄúTen thousand, and dollars, mind you, not even pounds. That‚Äôs the Greek for you. Humph! And what did you say to that?‚Äù

‚ÄúI said if I turned it over to him I‚Äôd expect the ten thousand.‚Äù

‚ÄúAh, yes, if! Nicely put, sir.‚Äù The fat man‚Äôs forehead squirmed in a flesh-blurred frown. ‚ÄúThey must know,‚Äù he said only partly aloud, then: ‚ÄúDo they? Do they know what the bird is, sir? What was your impression?‚Äù

‚ÄúI can‚Äôt help you there,‚Äù Spade confessed. ‚ÄúThere‚Äôs not much to go by. Cairo didn‚Äôt say he did and he didn‚Äôt say he didn‚Äôt. She said she didn‚Äôt, but I took it for granted that she was lying.‚Äù

‚ÄúThat was not an injudicious thing to do,‚Äù the fat man said, but his mind was obviously not on his words. He scratched his head. He frowned until his forehead was marked by raw red creases. He fidgeted in his chair as much as his size and the size of the chair permitted fidgeting. He shut his eyes, opened them suddenly‚ÅÝ‚Äîwide‚ÅÝ‚Äîand said to Spade: ‚ÄúMaybe they don‚Äôt.‚Äù His bulbous pink face slowly lost its worried frown and then, more quickly, took on an expression of ineffable happiness. ‚ÄúIf they don‚Äôt,‚Äù he cried, and again: ‚ÄúIf they don‚Äôt I‚Äôm the only one in the whole wide sweet world who does!‚Äù

Spade drew his lips back in a tight smile. ‚ÄúI‚Äôm glad I came to the right place,‚Äù he said.

The fat man smiled too, but somewhat vaguely. Happiness had gone out of his face, though he continued to smile, and caution had come into his eyes. His face was a watchful-eyed smiling mask held up between his thoughts and Spade. His eyes, avoiding Spade‚Äôs, shifted to the glass at Spade‚Äôs elbow. His face brightened. ‚ÄúBy Gad, sir,‚Äù he said, ‚Äúyour glass is empty.‚Äù He got up and went to the table and clattered glasses and siphon and bottle mixing two drinks.

Spade was immobile in his chair until the fat man, with a flourish and a bow and a jocular ‚ÄúAh, sir, this kind of medicine will never hurt you!‚Äù had handed him his refilled glass. Then Spade rose and stood close to the fat man, looking down at him, and Spade‚Äôs eyes were hard and bright. He raised his glass. His voice was deliberate, challenging: ‚ÄúHere‚Äôs to plain speaking and clear understanding.‚Äù

The fat man chuckled and they drank. The fat man sat down. He held his glass against his belly with both hands and smiled up at Spade. He said: ‚ÄúWell, sir, it‚Äôs surprising, but it well may be a fact that neither of them does know exactly what that bird is, and that nobody in all this whole wide sweet world knows what it is, saving and excepting only your humble servant, Casper Gutman, Esquire.‚Äù

‚ÄúSwell.‚Äù Spade stood with legs apart, one hand in his trousers-pocket, the other holding his glass. ‚ÄúWhen you‚Äôve told me there‚Äôll only be two of us who know.‚Äù

‚ÄúMathematically correct, sir‚Äù‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe fat man‚Äôs eyes twinkled‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äúbut‚Äù‚ÅÝ‚Äîhis smile spread‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚ÄúI don‚Äôt know for certain that I‚Äôm going to tell you.‚Äù

‚ÄúDon‚Äôt be a damned fool,‚Äù Spade said patiently. ‚ÄúYou know what it is. I know where it is. That‚Äôs why we‚Äôre here.‚Äù

‚ÄúWell, sir, where is it?‚Äù

Spade ignored the question.

The fat man bunched his lips, raised his eyebrows, and cocked his head a little to the left. ‚ÄúYou see,‚Äù he said blandly, ‚ÄúI must tell you what I know, but you will not tell me what you know. That is hardly equitable, sir. No, no, I do not think we can do business along those lines.‚Äù

Spade‚Äôs face became pale and hard. He spoke rapidly in a low furious voice: ‚ÄúThink again and think fast. I told that punk of yours that you‚Äôd have to talk to me before you got through. I‚Äôll tell you now that you‚Äôll do your talking today or you are through. What are you wasting my time for? You and your lousy secret! Christ! I know exactly what that stuff is that they keep in the subtreasury vaults, but what good does that do me? I can get along without you. God damn you! Maybe you could have got along without me if you‚Äôd kept clear of me. You can‚Äôt now. Not in San Francisco. You‚Äôll come in or you‚Äôll get out‚ÅÝ‚Äîand you‚Äôll do it today.‚Äù

He turned and with angry heedlessness tossed his glass at the table. The glass struck the wood, burst apart, and splashed its contents and glittering fragments over table and floor. Spade, deaf and blind to the crash, wheeled to confront the fat man again.

The fat man paid no more attention to the glass‚Äôs fate than Spade did: lips pursed, eyebrows raised, head cocked a little to the left, he had maintained his pink-faced blandness throughout Spade‚Äôs angry speech, and he maintained it now.

Spade, still furious, said: ‚ÄúAnd another thing, I don‚Äôt want‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù

The door to Spade‚Äôs left opened. The boy who had admitted Spade came in. He shut the door, stood in front of it with his hands flat against his flanks, and looked at Spade. The boy‚Äôs eyes were wide open and dark with wide pupils. Their gaze ran over Spade‚Äôs body from shoulders to knees, and up again to settle on the handkerchief whose maroon border peeped from the breast-pocket of Spade‚Äôs brown coat.

‚ÄúAnother thing,‚Äù Spade repeated, glaring at the boy: ‚ÄúKeep that gunsel away from me while you‚Äôre making up your mind. I‚Äôll kill him. I don‚Äôt like him. He makes me nervous. I‚Äôll kill him the first time he gets in my way. I won‚Äôt give him an even break. I won‚Äôt give him a chance. I‚Äôll kill him.‚Äù

The boy‚Äôs lips twitched in a shadowy smile. He neither raised his eyes nor spoke.

The fat man said tolerantly: ‚ÄúWell, sir, I must say you have a most violent temper.‚Äù

‚ÄúTemper?‚Äù Spade laughed crazily. He crossed to the chair on which he had dropped his hat, picked up the hat, and set it on his head. He held out a long arm that ended in a thick forefinger pointing at the fat man‚Äôs belly. His angry voice filled the room. ‚ÄúThink it over and think like hell. You‚Äôve got till five-thirty to do it in. Then you‚Äôre either in or out, for keeps.‚Äù He let his arm drop, scowled at the bland fat man for a moment, scowled at the boy, and went to the door through which he had entered. When he opened the door he turned and said harshly: ‚ÄúFive-thirty‚ÅÝ‚Äîthen the curtain.‚Äù

The boy, staring at Spade‚Äôs chest, repeated the two words he had twice spoken in the Belvedere lobby. His voice was not loud. It was bitter.

Spade went out and slammed the door.
